subject: Chemistry
text: Complex compounds or coordination compounds are those molecular compounds which retain their identify in solid as well as in solution are known as complex compounds. Example, K 4 [Fe(CN) 6 ] + H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4K + (aq) + [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4- (aq)
text_html:
Complex compounds or coordination compounds are those molecular compounds which retain their identify in solid as well as in solution are known as complex compounds.
Example, K4[Fe(CN)6] + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4K+(aq) + [Fe(CN)6]4-(aq)

subject: Chemistry
text: History of Coordination Compounds Alfred Werner was the pioneer in formulating ideas about the structures of coordination compounds during the late 19th century. He prepared and studied numerous such compounds, analyzing their behaviors through basic experimental methods.
text_html: History of Coordination Compounds Alfred Werner was the pioneer in formulating ideas about the structures of coordination compounds during the late 19th century. He prepared and studied numerous such compounds, analyzing their behaviors through basic experimental methods.

subject: Chemistry
text: Primary and Secondary Valence Werner proposed two types of valences in metal ions: - *Primary Valence*: Ionizable; satisfied by negative ions. - *Secondary Valence*: Non-ionizable; satisfied by neutral molecules or negative ions. The number of these bonds is called the coordination number.
text_html: Primary and Secondary Valence Werner proposed two types of valences in metal ions: - *Primary Valence*: Ionizable; satisfied by negative ions. - *Secondary Valence*: Non-ionizable; satisfied by neutral molecules or negative ions. The number of these bonds is called the coordination number.

subject: Chemistry
text: Binary Compound Valences The primary valence of some binary compounds includes: - 3 for {tex} {CrCl}_3 {/tex} - 2 for {tex} {CoCl}_2 {/tex} - 2 for {tex} {PdCl}_2 {/tex}
text_html: Binary Compound Valences The primary valence of some binary compounds includes: - 3 for {tex} {CrCl}_3 {/tex} - 2 for {tex} {CoCl}_2 {/tex} - 2 for {tex} {PdCl}_2 {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Cobalt(III) Chloride-Ammonia Complexes Different ammonia-cobalt(III) chloride complexes produce varying volumes of AgCl when reacted: - Yellow (3 mol AgCl) - Purple (2 mol AgCl) - Green and Violet (1 mol AgCl each)
text_html: Cobalt(III) Chloride-Ammonia Complexes Different ammonia-cobalt(III) chloride complexes produce varying volumes of AgCl when reacted: - Yellow (3 mol AgCl) - Purple (2 mol AgCl) - Green and Violet (1 mol AgCl each)

subject: Chemistry
text: Observations on Cobalt Complexes The invariability in these complexes suggests six groups always bond to the cobalt, remaining undissociated in reaction conditions.
text_html: Observations on Cobalt Complexes The invariability in these complexes suggests six groups always bond to the cobalt, remaining undissociated in reaction conditions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Secondary Valence Formulae Formulas for cobalt complexes show different observed electrolyte conductivities: - Yellow is a 1:3 electrolyte - Purple is a 1:2 electrolyte - Green and Violet are 1:1 electrolytes
text_html: Secondary Valence Formulae Formulas for cobalt complexes show different observed electrolyte conductivities: - Yellow is a 1:3 electrolyte - Purple is a 1:2 electrolyte - Green and Violet are 1:1 electrolytes

subject: Chemistry
text: Coordination Polyhedra and Theories The spatial arrangements, called coordination polyhedra, vary: - Most common are octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar shapes. - Octahedral examples: {tex} {Co} ight)^{3+} {/tex} complexes - Tetrahedral example: {tex} ight[{Ni}({CO})_4 ight] {/tex}
text_html: Coordination Polyhedra and Theories The spatial arrangements, called coordination polyhedra, vary: - Most common are octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar shapes. - Octahedral examples: {tex} {Co} ight)^{3+} {/tex} complexes - Tetrahedral example: {tex} ight[{Ni}({CO})_4 ight] {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Understanding Isomers Compounds with the same formula but different properties, like {tex} {CoCl}_3 .4 {NH}_3 {/tex} , are termed isomers. They exhibit distinct colors and conductivity properties even with identical empirical formulas.
text_html: Understanding Isomers Compounds with the same formula but different properties, like {tex} {CoCl}_3 .4 {NH}_3 {/tex} , are termed isomers. They exhibit distinct colors and conductivity properties even with identical empirical formulas.

subject: Chemistry
text: Double Salts vs. Complexes - *Double Salts*: Dissociate completely into simple ions (e.g., Carnallite, Mohr's salt). - *Complexes*: Do not dissociate into simple ions (e.g., {tex} ight[{Fe}({CN})_6 ight]^{4-} {/tex} ).
text_html: Double Salts vs. Complexes - *Double Salts*: Dissociate completely into simple ions (e.g., Carnallite, Mohr's salt). - *Complexes*: Do not dissociate into simple ions (e.g., {tex} ight[{Fe}({CN})_6 ight]^{4-} {/tex}).

subject: Chemistry
text: Postulates of Coordination Compounds Werner's main postulates include: 1. Two types of linkages - primary and secondary 2. Ionizable primary valences satisfied by negative ions 3. Non-ionizable secondary valences indicate coordination number 4. Specific spatial arrangements of ions/groups bound by secondary linkages
text_html: Postulates of Coordination Compounds Werner's main postulates include: 1. Two types of linkages - primary and secondary 2. Ionizable primary valences satisfied by negative ions 3. Non-ionizable secondary valences indicate coordination number 4. Specific spatial arrangements of ions/groups bound by secondary linkages

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Types of Complex Anionic complex K 3 [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3K + + [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3- Anionic complex Cationic complex [CoCl 2 (en) 2 ]Cl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} [CoCl 2 (en) 2 ] + + Cl - Cationic complex Neutral complex [Ni(CO) 4 ] Neutral complex
text_html:
Types of Complex

  1. Anionic complex K3[Fe(C2O4)3] {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 3K+ + [Fe(C2O4)3]3- Anionic complex
  2. Cationic complex [CoCl2(en)2]Cl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} [CoCl2(en)2]+ + Cl- Cationic complex
  3. Neutral complex [Ni(CO)4] Neutral complex

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Ligands: The ions or molecules bound to the central atom/ion in the coordination entity are called ligands.
text_html: Ligands: The ions or molecules bound to the central atom/ion in the coordination entity are called ligands.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Type of Complex Unidentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through a single donor atom. e.g. H 2 O, NH 3 , CO, Cl - , NH 2 - etc.
text_html:
Type of Complex
Unidentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through a single donor atom. e.g. H2O, NH3, CO, Cl-, NH2- etc.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Bidentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through two donor atom. Example: {tex}\underset{Oxalate\;ion}{\underset{COO^-}{\underset\vert COO^-}}\;\;\underset{Ethane-1,2-diamine}{\underset{CH_2-NH_2}{\;\underset\vert CH_2-NH_2}}{/tex} Oxalate ion Ethane -1, 2- diamine
text_html:
Bidentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through two donor atom.
Example: {tex}\underset{Oxalate\;ion}{\underset{COO^-}{\underset\vert COO^-}}\;\;\underset{Ethane-1,2-diamine}{\underset{CH_2-NH_2}{\;\underset\vert CH_2-NH_2}}{/tex}
Oxalate ion Ethane -1, 2- diamine

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Polydentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through several donor atoms. e.g., ethylene diamine tetraacetate ion [EDTA] 4- .
text_html: Polydentate: a ligand which is bound to a metal ion through several donor atoms. e.g., ethylene diamine tetraacetate ion [EDTA]4-.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Ambidentate ligands , which can bind through two different donor sites but binds through one site only eg., {tex}-\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} & -ONO - SCN - & SNC - CN - & NC -
text_html:
Ambidentate ligands, which can bind through two different donor sites but binds through one site only
eg., {tex}-\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}{/tex} & -ONO-
SCN- & SNC-
CN- & NC-

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Chelate ligands, these may be a di-or polydentate ligand which form closed ring with central metal ion. Closed ring is known as chelate ring. More the number of chelate ring in complex, complex will be more stable. The number of such ligating groups is called the denticity.
text_html: Chelate ligands, these may be a di-or polydentate ligand which form closed ring with central metal ion. Closed ring is known as chelate ring. More the number of chelate ring in complex, complex will be more stable. The number of such ligating groups is called the denticity.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Homoleptic Complexes Heteroleptic Complexes Complexes in which a metal is bound to only one kind of donor groups are known as homoleptic. Complexes in which a metal is bound to more than one kind of donor groups are known as heteroleptic. e.g. [Co(NH) 6 ] 3+ e.g. [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ] +
text_html:
Homoleptic Complexes Heteroleptic Complexes
Complexes in which a metal is bound to only one kind of donor groups are known as homoleptic. Complexes in which a metal is bound to more than one kind of donor groups are known as heteroleptic.
e.g. [Co(NH)6]3+ e.g. [Co(NH3)4Cl2]+

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Coordination Entity A coordination entity consists of a central metal atom or ion bonded to a fixed number of ions or molecules. Examples include: {tex} \left[{CoCl}_3\left({NH}_3\right)_3\right] {/tex} : Cobalt ion surrounded by ammonia molecules and chloride ions. {tex} \left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right] {/tex} , {tex} \left[{PtCl}_2\left({NH}_3\right)_2\right] {/tex} , {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex} , {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex} .
text_html:
Coordination Entity
A coordination entity consists of a central metal atom or ion bonded to a fixed number of ions or molecules. Examples include:

  • {tex} \left[{CoCl}_3\left({NH}_3\right)_3\right] {/tex}: Cobalt ion surrounded by ammonia molecules and chloride ions.
  • {tex} \left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right] {/tex}, {tex} \left[{PtCl}_2\left({NH}_3\right)_2\right] {/tex},
    {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex}, {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Central Atom/Ion The central atom/ion has ions/groups arranged around it. Examples with their central ions: {tex} \left[{NiCl}_2\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_4\right] {/tex} : {tex} {Ni}^{2+} {/tex} . {tex} \left[{CoCl}\left({NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+} {/tex} : {tex} {Co}^{3+} {/tex} . {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{3-} {/tex} : {tex} {Fe}^{3+} {/tex} . Central ions are also referred to as Lewis acids.
text_html:
Central Atom/Ion
The central atom/ion has ions/groups arranged around it.
Examples with their central ions:

  • {tex} \left[{NiCl}_2\left({H}_2 {O}\right)_4\right] {/tex}: {tex} {Ni}^{2+} {/tex}.
  • {tex} \left[{CoCl}\left({NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{2+} {/tex}: {tex} {Co}^{3+} {/tex}.
  • {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{3-} {/tex}: {tex} {Fe}^{3+} {/tex}.

Central ions are also referred to as Lewis acids.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Ligands Ligands are ions/molecules bound to the central atom, classified as: Unidentate: Single donor atom (e.g., {tex}{Cl}^{-}{/tex} ). Didentate: Two donor atoms (e.g., {tex} {H}_2 {NCH}_2 {CH}_2 {NH}_2 {/tex} ). Polydentate: Multiple donor atoms (e.g., {tex} {N}\left({CH}_2 {CH}_2 {NH}_2\right)_3 {/tex} ). Ligands can be chelate or ambidentate, facilitating complex stability.
text_html:
Ligands
Ligands are ions/molecules bound to the central atom, classified as:

  • Unidentate: Single donor atom (e.g., {tex}{Cl}^{-}{/tex}).
  • Didentate: Two donor atoms (e.g., {tex} {H}_2 {NCH}_2 {CH}_2 {NH}_2 {/tex}).
  • Polydentate: Multiple donor atoms (e.g., {tex} {N}\left({CH}_2 {CH}_2 {NH}_2\right)_3 {/tex}).

Ligands can be chelate or ambidentate, facilitating complex stability.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Coordination Number This number is the count of ligand donor atoms bonded to the metal ion. {tex} \left[{PtCl}_6\right]^{2-} {/tex} : Coordination number of Pt is 6. {tex} \left[{Ni}\left({NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+} {/tex} : Coordination number of Ni is 4. Coordination number is determined only by sigma bonds.
text_html:
Coordination Number
This number is the count of ligand donor atoms bonded to the metal ion.

  • {tex} \left[{PtCl}_6\right]^{2-} {/tex}: Coordination number of Pt is 6.
  • {tex} \left[{Ni}\left({NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+} {/tex}: Coordination number of Ni is 4.

Coordination number is determined only by sigma bonds.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Coordination Sphere The central atom/ion and its ligands are enclosed in square brackets to form the coordination sphere, with ionizable groups outside. Example: In {tex} {K}_4\left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right] {/tex} , coordination sphere is {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex} , and the counter ion is {tex} {K}^{+} {/tex} .
text_html:
Coordination Sphere
The central atom/ion and its ligands are enclosed in square brackets to form the coordination sphere, with ionizable groups outside.
Example: In {tex} {K}_4\left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right] {/tex}, coordination sphere is {tex} \left[{Fe}({CN})_6\right]^{4-} {/tex}, and the counter ion is {tex} {K}^{+} {/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Coordination Polyhedron The geometric arrangement of ligand atoms attached to the central atom forms a coordination polyhedron. Octahedral: {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex} . Tetrahedral: {tex} \left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right] {/tex} . Square planar: {tex} \left[{PtCl}_4\right]^{2-} {/tex} .
text_html:
Coordination Polyhedron
The geometric arrangement of ligand atoms attached to the central atom forms a coordination polyhedron.

  • Octahedral: {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex}.
  • Tetrahedral: {tex} \left[{Ni}({CO})_4\right] {/tex}.
  • Square planar: {tex} \left[{PtCl}_4\right]^{2-} {/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Oxidation Number of Central Atom Represents the hypothetical charge the central atom would have if all ligands were removed with their electron pairs. Notational Example: {tex} \left[{Cu}({CN})_4\right]^{3-} {/tex} : Oxidation number of copper is +1, denoted as {tex} {Cu}({I}) {/tex} .
text_html:
Oxidation Number of Central Atom
Represents the hypothetical charge the central atom would have if all ligands were removed with their electron pairs. Notational Example:

  • {tex} \left[{Cu}({CN})_4\right]^{3-} {/tex}: Oxidation number of copper is +1, denoted as {tex} {Cu}({I}) {/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Some Important Terms Pertaining To Coordination Compounds
text: Homoleptic and Heteroleptic Complexes Defines the diversity of donor groups bonded to the metal. Homoleptic: Bound to one kind of group (e.g., {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex} ). Heteroleptic: Bound to multiple kinds of donor groups (e.g., {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_4 {Cl}_2\right]^{+} {/tex} ).
text_html:
Homoleptic and Heteroleptic Complexes
Defines the diversity of donor groups bonded to the metal.

  • Homoleptic: Bound to one kind of group (e.g., {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+} {/tex}).
  • Heteroleptic: Bound to multiple kinds of donor groups (e.g., {tex} \left[{Co}\left({NH}_3\right)_4 {Cl}_2\right]^{+} {/tex}).

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds Neutral Complex [Pt(NH 3 ) 2 Cl(NO 2 )] Diamminechloridonitrito -N-platinum (II) (I) Name of ligands in an alphabetical order (II) Central metal atom and oxidation state
text_html:
Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds
Neutral Complex
[Pt(NH3)2Cl(NO2)]
Diamminechloridonitrito -N-platinum (II)
(I) Name of ligands in an alphabetical order
(II) Central metal atom and oxidation state

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds Anionic Complex K 3 [Fe(CN) 6 ] Postassium hexacyanatoferrate (III) Name of ionisable metal and oxidation state Name of ligand in an alphabetical order Central metal atom + ate and oxidation state
text_html:
Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds
Anionic Complex
K3[Fe(CN)6]
Postassium hexacyanatoferrate (III)

  1. Name of ionisable metal and oxidation state
  2. Name of ligand in an alphabetical order
  3. Central metal atom + ate and oxidation state

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds Cationic Complex [Cr(NH 3 ) 3 (H 2 O) 3 ]Cl 2 Triamminetriaquachromium(III) chloride Prefixes mono, di, tri, etc. are used to indicate the number of the individual ligands and ligands are named in an alphabetical order. Central metal atom and oxidation state indicated by Roman numerals in parenthesis. Name of ionisable anion.
text_html:
Nomenclature of Coordination Compounds
Cationic Complex
[Cr(NH3)3(H2O)3]Cl2
Triamminetriaquachromium(III) chloride

  1. Prefixes mono, di, tri, etc. are used to indicate the number of the individual ligands and ligands are named in an alphabetical order.
  2. Central metal atom and oxidation state indicated by Roman numerals in parenthesis.
  3. Name of ionisable anion.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nomenclature Importance Nomenclature ensures that coordination compounds have unambiguous systematic names as per the IUPAC guidelines.
text_html:
Nomenclature Importance
Nomenclature ensures that coordination compounds have unambiguous systematic names as per the IUPAC guidelines.

subject: Chemistry
text: Formulas of Mononuclear Coordination Entities The central atom is listed first. List ligands in alphabetical order, their charge doesn't affect ordering. Enclose the entire coordination entity's formula in square brackets. Indicate the charge outside the brackets using a right superscript. The charge of cation(s) balances with the anion(s).
text_html:
Formulas of Mononuclear Coordination Entities
  • The central atom is listed first.
  • List ligands in alphabetical order, their charge doesn't affect ordering.
  • Enclose the entire coordination entity's formula in square brackets.
  • Indicate the charge outside the brackets using a right superscript.
  • The charge of cation(s) balances with the anion(s).

subject: Chemistry
text: Naming of Coordination Compounds Based on additive nomenclature principles. Cation named first, irrespective of charge. Ligands named alphabetically before the central atom/ion. Use -o suffix for anionic ligands; specific names for neutral ligands (e.g., aqua for H 2 O). Use mono, di, tri for ligand counts; bis, tris, tetrakis when ligands have numerical prefixes.
text_html:
Naming of Coordination Compounds
  • Based on additive nomenclature principles.
  • Cation named first, irrespective of charge.
  • Ligands named alphabetically before the central atom/ion.
  • Use -o suffix for anionic ligands; specific names for neutral ligands (e.g., aqua for H2O).
  • Use mono, di, tri for ligand counts; bis, tris, tetrakis when ligands have numerical prefixes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Oxidation State Indication Indicated by Roman numeral in parenthesis.
text_html:
Oxidation State Indication
  • Indicated by Roman numeral in parenthesis.

subject: Chemistry
text: Cation and Anion Naming If complex ion is a cation, name metal same as element. If complex ion is an anion, metal name ends with -ate. Latin names used for some metals in complex anions (e.g., ferrate for Fe).
text_html:
Cation and Anion Naming
  • If complex ion is a cation, name metal same as element.
  • If complex ion is an anion, metal name ends with -ate.
  • Latin names used for some metals in complex anions (e.g., ferrate for Fe).

subject: Chemistry
text: Example 1 Naming History Formula: [Cr(NH 3 ) 3 (H 2 O) 3 ]Cl 3 Name: Triamminetri aqua chromium(III) chloride. Traits: Cation inside brackets. Neutral ligands mean Cr's oxidation is +3.
text_html:
Example 1 Naming History
Formula: [Cr(NH3)3(H2O)3]Cl3
Name: Triamminetri aqua chromium(III) chloride. Traits:
  • Cation inside brackets.
  • Neutral ligands mean Cr's oxidation is +3.

subject: Chemistry
text: Example 2 Naming Details Formula: [Co(H 2 NCH 2 CH 2 NH 2 ) 3 ] 2 (SO 4 ) 3 Name: Tris(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III) sulphate. Concepts: Contains counter anion (sulphate). Co oxidation state of +3 with neutral ligands.
text_html:
Example 2 Naming Details
Formula: [Co(H2NCH2CH2NH2)3]2(SO4)3
Name: Tris(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III) sulphate. Concepts:
  • Contains counter anion (sulphate).
  • Co oxidation state of +3 with neutral ligands.

subject: Chemistry
text: Example 3 Naming Details Formula: [Ag(NH 3 ) 2 ][Ag(CN) 2 ] Name: Diamminesilver(I) dicyanidoargentate(I). Points: Both the ligands are specified alphabetically before metal names.
text_html:
Example 3 Naming Details
Formula: [Ag(NH3)2][Ag(CN)2]
Name: Diamminesilver(I) dicyanidoargentate(I). Points:
  • Both the ligands are specified alphabetically before metal names.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ligand Placement in Formulas Enclose polyatomic ligands in parentheses. No spaces between metal and ligands within the coordination sphere.
text_html:
Ligand Placement in Formulas
  • Enclose polyatomic ligands in parentheses.
  • No spaces between metal and ligands within the coordination sphere.

subject: Chemistry
text: Complex Ion Charge Representation When isolated, indicate charge outside brackets as a right superscript. Number precedes charge sign (example: 3+).
text_html:
Complex Ion Charge Representation
  • When isolated, indicate charge outside brackets as a right superscript.
  • Number precedes charge sign (example: 3+).

subject: Chemistry
text: Stereo Isomerism It occurs due to different arrangements of ligands around central metal atom. It is of two types: Geometrical Isomerism and Optical Isomerism
text_html:
Stereo Isomerism
It occurs due to different arrangements of ligands around central metal atom. It is of two types: Geometrical Isomerism and Optical Isomerism

subject: Chemistry
text: Optical Isomerism It arises when mirror images cannot be superimposed on one another. These mirror images are called as enantiomers. The two forms are called dextro (d) and laevo (l). Optical isomerism is common in octahedral complexes but at least one didentate ligand must be present. e.g., [Co(en) 3 ] 3+ , [PtCl 2 (en) 2 ] 2+ etc.
text_html:
Optical Isomerism

  • It arises when mirror images cannot be superimposed on one another. These mirror images are called as enantiomers. The two forms are called dextro (d) and laevo (l).
  • Optical isomerism is common in octahedral complexes but at least one didentate ligand must be present.
    e.g., [Co(en)3]3+, [PtCl2(en)2]2+ etc.

subject: Chemistry
text: Geometrical Isomerism: It arises in heteroleptic complexes due to different possible geometric arrangements of the ligands. Important examples of this behavior are found in square planar and octahedral complexes. Important examples of this behaviour are found in Square Planar.
text_html: Geometrical Isomerism: It arises in heteroleptic complexes due to different possible geometric arrangements of the ligands. Important examples of this behavior are found in square planar and octahedral complexes. Important examples of this behaviour are found in Square Planar.

subject: Chemistry
text: Geometrical Isomerism Important examples of this behaviour are found in Square Planar. Square planar complex of formula [MX 2 L 2 ](X and L are unidentate), The two ligands X may be arranged adjacent to each other in a cis isomer, or opposite to each other in a trans isomer. e.g., [Pt(NH 3 ) 2 Cl 2 ] Square planar complex of the type [MABXL](where A, B, X, L are unidentates) shows three isomers- two cis and one trans. Such isomerism is not possible for terahedral geometry. e.g., [Pt(NH 3 )(Br)(Cl)(Py)] Octahedral complexes of formula [MX 2 A 2 ] where X are unidentates and A are didentate and form cis and trans isomers. e.g., [CoCl 2 (en) 2 ] Octahedral complexes of formula [MX 2 L 4 ] in which the two ligands X may be oriented cis or trans to each other. e.g., [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ] +
text_html:
Geometrical Isomerism
Important examples of this behaviour are found in Square Planar.
Square planar complex of formula [MX2L2](X and L are unidentate), The two ligands X may be arranged adjacent to each other in a cis isomer, or opposite to each other in a trans isomer. e.g., [Pt(NH3)2Cl2]
Square planar complex of the type [MABXL](where A, B, X, L are unidentates) shows three isomers- two cis and one trans. Such isomerism is not possible for terahedral geometry. e.g., [Pt(NH3)(Br)(Cl)(Py)]
Octahedral complexes of formula [MX2A2] where X are unidentates and A are didentate and form cis and trans isomers.
e.g., [CoCl2(en)2]
Octahedral complexes of formula [MX2L4] in which the two ligands X may be oriented cis or trans to each other.
e.g., [Co(NH3)4Cl2]+

subject: Chemistry
text: Structural Isomerism Linkage isomerism, arises in a coordination compound containing ambidentate ligand. e.g., [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (NO 2 )]Cl 2 [Co(NH 3 ) 5 (ONO)]Cl 2 Coordination isomerism, arises from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions present in a complex. e.g., [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ][Cr(CN) 6 ], [Cr(NH 3 ) 6 ][Co(CN) 6 ] Ionisation isomerism, when the ionisable anion exhange with anion ligand. e.g., [Co(NH 3 ) 5 SO 4 ]Br and [Co(NH 3 ) 5 Br]SO 4 Solvate isomerism , is also known as hydrate isomerism . In this case water is involved as a solvent. e.g., [Cr(H 2 O) 6 ]Cl 3 , [Cr(H 2 O) 4 Cl 2 ]Cl. 2H 2 O
text_html:
Structural Isomerism

  • Linkage isomerism, arises in a coordination compound containing ambidentate ligand. e.g., [Co(NH3)5(NO2)]Cl2 [Co(NH3)5(ONO)]Cl2
  • Coordination isomerism, arises from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions present in a complex. e.g., [Co(NH3)6][Cr(CN)6], [Cr(NH3)6][Co(CN)6]
  • Ionisation isomerism, when the ionisable anion exhange with anion ligand. e.g., [Co(NH3)5SO4]Br and [Co(NH3)5Br]SO4
  • Solvate isomerism, is also known as hydrate isomerism. In this case water is involved as a solvent. e.g., [Cr(H2O)6]Cl3, [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]Cl. 2H2O

subject: Chemistry
text: Isomers Overview Isomers are compounds with the same chemical formula but different atom arrangements, leading to different chemical and physical properties. Two principal types of isomerism in coordination compounds: Stereoisomerism Structural isomerism
text_html:
Isomers Overview

Isomers are compounds with the same chemical formula but different atom arrangements, leading to different chemical and physical properties.

  • Two principal types of isomerism in coordination compounds:
    • Stereoisomerism
    • Structural isomerism

subject: Chemistry
text: Stereoisomerism Stereoisomers share the same chemical formula and bonds but have different spatial arrangements. Includes: Geometric isomerism Optical isomerism
text_html:
Stereoisomerism

Stereoisomers share the same chemical formula and bonds but have different spatial arrangements.

  • Includes:
    • Geometric isomerism
    • Optical isomerism

subject: Chemistry
text: Geometric Isomerism Occurs in heteroleptic complexes due to different possible geometric ligand arrangements. Common in complexes with coordination numbers 4 and 6. Types of geometric isomers: Cis isomer Trans isomer Facial (fac) isomer Meridional (mer) isomer
text_html:
Geometric Isomerism

Occurs in heteroleptic complexes due to different possible geometric ligand arrangements.

  • Common in complexes with coordination numbers 4 and 6.
  • Types of geometric isomers:
    • Cis isomer
    • Trans isomer
    • Facial (fac) isomer
    • Meridional (mer) isomer

subject: Chemistry
text: Optical Isomerism Involves non-superimposable mirror images called enantiomers. Chiral molecules result in different forms based on the direction they rotate polarized light. Common in octahedral complexes with didentate ligands.
text_html:
Optical Isomerism

Involves non-superimposable mirror images called enantiomers.

  • Chiral molecules result in different forms based on the direction they rotate polarized light.
  • Common in octahedral complexes with didentate ligands.

subject: Chemistry
text: Structural Isomerism Structural isomers have different bonds. Includes: Linkage isomerism Coordination isomerism Ionisation isomerism Solvate isomerism
text_html:
Structural Isomerism

Structural isomers have different bonds.

  • Includes:
    • Linkage isomerism
    • Coordination isomerism
    • Ionisation isomerism
    • Solvate isomerism

subject: Chemistry
text: Linkage Isomerism Arises in coordination compounds with ambidentate ligands, binding differently within a molecule. Example: Thiocyanate ligand can bind through nitrogen or sulphur.
text_html:
Linkage Isomerism

Arises in coordination compounds with ambidentate ligands, binding differently within a molecule.

  • Example: Thiocyanate ligand can bind through nitrogen or sulphur.

subject: Chemistry
text: Coordination Isomerism Results from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions. Illustrated by complexes where ligands bind to different metal ions after rearrangement.
text_html:
Coordination Isomerism

Results from the interchange of ligands between cationic and anionic entities of different metal ions.

  • Illustrated by complexes where ligands bind to different metal ions after rearrangement.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ionisation Isomerism Occurs when a counter ion in a complex salt acts as a potential ligand and displaces a ligand. Illustrated by the swapping of positions between ligands and counter ions in complexes.
text_html:
Ionisation Isomerism

Occurs when a counter ion in a complex salt acts as a potential ligand and displaces a ligand.

  • Illustrated by the swapping of positions between ligands and counter ions in complexes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solvate Isomerism Solvate or 'hydrate' isomerism occurs when solvent molecules are bonded directly to a metal ion or present freely. Example: Transition between aqua complexes with different crystal lattice arrangements.
text_html:
Solvate Isomerism

Solvate or 'hydrate' isomerism occurs when solvent molecules are bonded directly to a metal ion or present freely.

  • Example: Transition between aqua complexes with different crystal lattice arrangements.

subject: Chemistry
text: Importance of Isomerism Understanding isomerism is crucial in coordination chemistry due to its effects on the properties and behavior of chemical compounds.
text_html:
Importance of Isomerism

Understanding isomerism is crucial in coordination chemistry due to its effects on the properties and behavior of chemical compounds.

subject: Chemistry
text: Octahedral coordination entities of the type [Ma 3 b 3 ] like [Co(NH 3 ) 3 (NO 2 ) 3 ]. If three donor atoms of the same ligands occupy adjacent positions at the corners of an octahedral face, we have the facial (fac) isomer. When the positions are around the meridian of the octahedron, we get the meridional (mer) isomer.
text_html: Octahedral coordination entities of the type [Ma3b3] like [Co(NH3)3(NO2)3]. If three donor atoms of the same ligands occupy adjacent positions at the corners of an octahedral face, we have the facial (fac) isomer. When the positions are around the meridian of the octahedron, we get the meridional (mer) isomer.

subject: Chemistry
text: Bonding in Coordination Compounds Werner's Theory In complex compounds, metal atom exhibit two types of valencies primary valency and secondary valency. Primary valencies are satisfied by anions only while secondary valencies are satisfied by ligands. Primary valency depends upon oxidation number of central metal atom while secondary valency represents coordination number of central metal atom. Primary valencies are ionisable and are non-directional while secondary valencies are non-ionisable and directional. Therefore, geometry of complex is decided by secondary valencies.
text_html:
Bonding in Coordination Compounds
Werner's Theory

  1. In complex compounds, metal atom exhibit two types of valencies primary valency and secondary valency.
  2. Primary valencies are satisfied by anions only while secondary valencies are satisfied by ligands. Primary valency depends upon oxidation number of central metal atom while secondary valency represents coordination number of central metal atom.
  3. Primary valencies are ionisable and are non-directional while secondary valencies are non-ionisable and directional. Therefore, geometry of complex is decided by secondary valencies.

subject: Chemistry
text: Bonding in Coordination Compounds Valence Bond Theory According to this theory, the metal atom or ion under the influence of ligands from inner orbital and outer orbital complex. These hybridized orbitals are allowed to overlap with ligand orbitals that can donate electron pairs for bonding.
text_html:
Bonding in Coordination Compounds
Valence Bond Theory
According to this theory, the metal atom or ion under the influence of ligands from inner orbital and outer orbital complex. These hybridized orbitals are allowed to overlap with ligand orbitals that can donate electron pairs for bonding.

subject: Chemistry
text: Six ligands (unidentate) (octahedral entity): Generally central atom belongs 3d series and ligands can be monodentate or didentate but coordination number should be six and shape of complexes will be octahedral and form two types of complexes. Two types of complexes Inner orbital complex and Outer orbital complexes.
text_html: Six ligands (unidentate) (octahedral entity): Generally central atom belongs 3d series and ligands can be monodentate or didentate but coordination number should be six and shape of complexes will be octahedral and form two types of complexes. Two types of complexes Inner orbital complex and Outer orbital complexes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Two types of complexes: Inner orbital complexes, Which are formed due to participation of (n-1)d orbitals in hybridisation is (d 2 sp 3 ) and shape of complex will be octahedral. Outer orbital complexes, Which are formed due to participation of nd orbitals in hybridisation is (sp 3 d 2 ).
text_html:
Two types of complexes:
Inner orbital complexes, Which are formed due to participation of (n-1)d orbitals in hybridisation is (d2sp3) and shape of complex will be octahedral.
Outer orbital complexes, Which are formed due to participation of nd orbitals in hybridisation is (sp3d2).

subject: Chemistry
text: Crystal Field Theory: The five d-orbitals are split into lower and higher energy level due to approach of ligands is known as crystal field theory. The five d-orbitals in a gaseous metal atom/ion have same energy.
text_html: Crystal Field Theory: The five d-orbitals are split into lower and higher energy level due to approach of ligands is known as crystal field theory. The five d-orbitals in a gaseous metal atom/ion have same energy.

subject: Chemistry
text: Crystal field splitting in octahedral coordination entities Energy separation is denoted by {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} (the subscript o is for octahedral). The energy of the two e_g orbitals (higher energy orbitals) will increase by (3/5) {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} . and that of the three t 2g (lower energy orbitals) will decrease by (2/5) {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} If {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}
text_html:
Crystal field splitting in octahedral coordination entities

  • Energy separation is denoted by {tex}\Delta_0{/tex} (the subscript o is for octahedral).
  • The energy of the two e_g orbitals (higher energy orbitals) will increase by (3/5) {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}. and that of the three t2g (lower energy orbitals) will decrease by (2/5) {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}
  • If {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}<P, the fourth electron enters one of the eg, orbitals giving the configuration {tex}t^3{ }_{2 g} e_g{ }^1{/tex}. Ligands for which {tex}\Delta_0{/tex}<P are known as weak field ligands and form high spin complexes.
  • If {tex}\Delta_o<\mathrm{P}{/tex}, it becomes more energeticallly favourable for the fourth electron to occupy {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{/tex} orbital with configuration {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^4 \mathrm{e}_8{ }^0{/tex}.
    Ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands which produce this effect are known as strong field ligands and from low spin complexes, where P represents the energy required for electron pairing in a single orbital.

subject: Chemistry
text: Crystal field splitting in tetrahedral coordination entities. In tetrahedral coordination entities, {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{t}}=(4 / 9) \Delta_0{/tex} . Consequently the orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing pairing, therefore, low spin configurations are rarely observed. Due to less crystal field stabilization energy, it is not possible to pair electrons and so all the tetrahedral complexes are high spin.
text_html:
Crystal field splitting in tetrahedral coordination entities.

  • In tetrahedral coordination entities, {tex}\Delta_{\mathrm{t}}=(4 / 9) \Delta_0{/tex}.
    Consequently the orbital splitting energies are not sufficiently large for forcing pairing, therefore, low spin configurations are rarely observed.
  • Due to less crystal field stabilization energy, it is not possible to pair electrons and so all the tetrahedral complexes are high spin.

subject: Chemistry
text: Colour in Coordination Compounds In complex compounds d-orbitals split in two sets t 2g and e g These have different energies. The difference in energies lies in visible region and electron jump from ground state t 2g level to higher state e g level. This is known as d-d transition and it is responsible for colour of coordination compounds. d-d transition takes place in d 1 to d 9 ions, so the ions having d 1 to d 9 configuration are coloured. On the other hand, the ions d 0 and d 10 configuration do not show d-d transition.
text_html:
Colour in Coordination Compounds

  • In complex compounds d-orbitals split in two sets t2g and eg These have different energies. The difference in energies lies in visible region and electron jump from ground state t2g level to higher state eg level. This is known as d-d transition and it is responsible for colour of coordination compounds.
  • d-d transition takes place in d1 to d9 ions, so the ions having d1 to d9 configuration are coloured. On the other hand, the ions d0 and d10 configuration do not show d-d transition.

subject: Chemistry
text: Coordination Compounds Concepts Bonding theories for coordination compounds include Valence Bond Theory (VBT), Crystal Field Theory (CFT), Ligand Field Theory (LFT), and Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT). Focus is placed on VBT and CFT.
text_html:
Coordination Compounds Concepts
Bonding theories for coordination compounds include Valence Bond Theory (VBT), Crystal Field Theory (CFT), Ligand Field Theory (LFT), and Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT). Focus is placed on VBT and CFT.

subject: Chemistry
text: Valence Bond Theory (VBT) VBT explains that metal ions use their orbitals for hybridisation to create equivalent orbitals. These include octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar shapes. Hybrid orbitals overlap with ligand orbitals for electron pair bonding.
text_html:
Valence Bond Theory (VBT)
VBT explains that metal ions use their orbitals for hybridisation to create equivalent orbitals. These include octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar shapes. Hybrid orbitals overlap with ligand orbitals for electron pair bonding.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hybridisation and Coordination Number - **Tetrahedral**: Coordination 4, sp3 hybridisation. - **Square Planar**: Coordination 4, dsp2 hybridisation. - **Trigonal bipyramidal**: Coordination 5, sp3d. - **Octahedral**: Coordination 6, sp3d2 or d2sp3.
text_html:
Hybridisation and Coordination Number
- **Tetrahedral**: Coordination 4, sp3 hybridisation.
- **Square Planar**: Coordination 4, dsp2 hybridisation.
- **Trigonal bipyramidal**: Coordination 5, sp3d.
- **Octahedral**: Coordination 6, sp3d2 or d2sp3.

subject: Chemistry
text: Magnetic Properties Determined through magnetic susceptibility. Agreements with maximum spin pairing observed with d6 ions. Explains diamagnetic and paramagnetic nature based on electron pairing.
text_html:
Magnetic Properties
Determined through magnetic susceptibility. Agreements with maximum spin pairing observed with d6 ions. Explains diamagnetic and paramagnetic nature based on electron pairing.

subject: Chemistry
text: Limitations of VBT Has several shortcomings: Assumptions-based Lacks quantitative interpretation of magnetic data and thermodynamic stability Fails to predict exact structures of complexes Does not differentiate between ligand strength
text_html:
Limitations of VBT
Has several shortcomings:
  • Assumptions-based
  • Lacks quantitative interpretation of magnetic data and thermodynamic stability
  • Fails to predict exact structures of complexes
  • Does not differentiate between ligand strength

subject: Chemistry
text: Crystal Field Theory (CFT) CFT considers metal-ligand bonds as ionic, originating from electrostatic interactions. For octahedral entities, d orbitals split into lower energy t2g and higher energy eg orbitals.
text_html:
Crystal Field Theory (CFT)
CFT considers metal-ligand bonds as ionic, originating from electrostatic interactions. For octahedral entities, d orbitals split into lower energy t2g and higher energy eg orbitals.

subject: Chemistry
text: Octahedral Split d orbitals experience an asymmetrical field, causing d orbitals to split due to ligand repulsion. Energy of eg orbitals increases, and t2g decreases. The difference is denoted by Δo.
text_html:
Octahedral Split
d orbitals experience an asymmetrical field, causing d orbitals to split due to ligand repulsion. Energy of eg orbitals increases, and t2g decreases. The difference is denoted by Δo.

subject: Chemistry
text: Tetrahedral Split For tetrahedral entities, d orbital splitting is inverted and smaller than octahedral. Δt = (4/9)Δo. Tetrahedral complexes lack 'g' subscript due to lack of central symmetry.
text_html:
Tetrahedral Split
For tetrahedral entities, d orbital splitting is inverted and smaller than octahedral. Δt = (4/9)Δo. Tetrahedral complexes lack 'g' subscript due to lack of central symmetry.

subject: Chemistry
text: Colour of Complexes The colour stems from the absorption of specific wavelengths as d electrons transition between energy levels, i.e., from t2g to eg. Complementary colours to the absorbed ones are observed.
text_html:
Colour of Complexes
The colour stems from the absorption of specific wavelengths as d electrons transition between energy levels, i.e., from t2g to eg. Complementary colours to the absorbed ones are observed.

subject: Chemistry
text: Limitations of CFT CFT does not predict ligand anion effect correctly. It assumes point charges and doesn’t consider covalent character, resolved through Ligand Field Theory.
text_html:
Limitations of CFT
CFT does not predict ligand anion effect correctly. It assumes point charges and doesn’t consider covalent character, resolved through Ligand Field Theory.

subject: Chemistry
text: Homoleptic Carbonyls The homoleptic carbonyls only contain carbonyl ligands and are common among transition metals.
text_html:
Homoleptic Carbonyls
The homoleptic carbonyls only contain carbonyl ligands and are common among transition metals.

subject: Chemistry
text: Structure of Tetracarbonylnickel(0) This compound exhibits a tetrahedral structure.
text_html:
Structure of Tetracarbonylnickel(0)
This compound exhibits a tetrahedral structure.

subject: Chemistry
text: Structure of Pentacarbonyliron(0) It has a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.
text_html:
Structure of Pentacarbonyliron(0)
It has a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.

subject: Chemistry
text: Hexacarbonyl Chromium(0) Features an octahedral structure.
text_html:
Hexacarbonyl Chromium(0)
Features an octahedral structure.

subject: Chemistry
text: Decacarbonyldimanganese (0) Composed of two square pyramidal Mn(CO)₅ units. Units are linked by an Mn-Mn bond.
text_html:
Decacarbonyldimanganese (0)
  • Composed of two square pyramidal Mn(CO)₅ units.
  • Units are linked by an Mn-Mn bond.

subject: Chemistry
text: Octacarbonyldicobalt(0) Has a Co-Co bond. Bond is bridged by two CO groups.
text_html:
Octacarbonyldicobalt(0)
  • Has a Co-Co bond.
  • Bond is bridged by two CO groups.

subject: Chemistry
text: Metal-Carbon Bond Nature Composed of both σ and π characters. σ Bond: Formed by donation of electrons from carbonyl carbon to the metal's vacant orbital.
text_html:
Metal-Carbon Bond Nature
  • Composed of both σ and π characters.
  • σ Bond: Formed by donation of electrons from carbonyl carbon to the metal's vacant orbital.

subject: Chemistry
text: π Bond Formation π bond arises from electron donation from a filled d orbital of the metal into the vacant π* orbital of CO.
text_html:
π Bond Formation
  • π bond arises from electron donation from a filled d orbital of the metal into the vacant π* orbital of CO.

subject: Chemistry
text: Synergic Effect Through metal to ligand bonding, a synergic effect is created. This effect strengthens the CO-metal bond.
text_html:
Synergic Effect
  • Through metal to ligand bonding, a synergic effect is created.
  • This effect strengthens the CO-metal bond.

subject: Chemistry
text: Visual Representations Figures 5.13 and 5.14 illustrate the structures and bonding.
text_html:
Visual Representations
  • Figures 5.13 and 5.14 illustrate the structures and bonding.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds. Hardness of water is estimated by simple titration with Na 2 EDTA. The Ca 2+ and Mg 2+ ions form stable complexes with EDTA. Some important extraction processes of metals, like those of silver and gold make use of complex formation. Similarly, purification of metals can be achieved through formation and subsequent decomposition of their coordination compounds. For example, impure nickel is converted to [Ni(CO) 4 ], which is decomposed to yield pure nickel. Coordination compounds are used as catalysts for many industrial processes. Examples include rhodium.
text_html:
Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds.

  • Hardness of water is estimated by simple titration with Na2EDTA. The Ca2+ and Mg2+ ions form stable complexes with EDTA.
  • Some important extraction processes of metals, like those of silver and gold make use of complex formation.
  • Similarly, purification of metals can be achieved through formation and subsequent decomposition of their coordination compounds. For example, impure nickel is converted to [Ni(CO)4], which is decomposed to yield pure nickel.
  • Coordination compounds are used as catalysts for many industrial processes. Examples include rhodium.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Coordination Compounds and their Importance Coordination compounds are crucial in minerals, plants, and animals, playing roles in analytical chemistry, metallurgy, biology, industry, and medicine.
text_html:
Coordination Compounds and their Importance
Coordination compounds are crucial in minerals, plants, and animals, playing roles in analytical chemistry, metallurgy, biology, industry, and medicine.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Analytical Chemistry Applications Used in qualitative and quantitative analysis. Metal ion detection through color reactions with ligands like EDTA and DMG.
text_html:
Analytical Chemistry Applications
  • Used in qualitative and quantitative analysis.
  • Metal ion detection through color reactions with ligands like EDTA and DMG.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Water Hardness Measurement Measured using {tex} {Na}_2 {/tex} EDTA titration. Forms stable complexes with {tex} {Ca}^{2+} {/tex} and {tex} {Mg}^{2+} {/tex} ions.
text_html:
Water Hardness Measurement

  • Measured using {tex} {Na}_2 {/tex} EDTA titration.
  • Forms stable complexes with {tex} {Ca}^{2+} {/tex} and {tex} {Mg}^{2+} {/tex} ions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Metal Extraction and Purification Gold and silver extraction utilizes complex formation. Example: Gold forms {tex} ext{[Au(CN)}_2{]}^{-} {/tex} with cyanide. Metal purification through coordination compounds is possible.
text_html:
Metal Extraction and Purification

  • Gold and silver extraction utilizes complex formation.
  • Example: Gold forms {tex} ext{[Au(CN)}_2{]}^{-} {/tex} with cyanide.
  • Metal purification through coordination compounds is possible.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Biological System Significance Chlorophyll, a coordination compound of magnesium. Haemoglobin is a coordination compound of iron. Vitamin {tex} {B}_{12} {/tex} , a coordination compound of cobalt.
text_html:
Biological System Significance

  • Chlorophyll, a coordination compound of magnesium.
  • Haemoglobin is a coordination compound of iron.
  • Vitamin {tex} {B}_{12} {/tex}, a coordination compound of cobalt.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Catalysts in Industry Coordination compounds like Wilkinson catalyst ( {tex} ext{[Ph}_3 ext{P}_3 ext{RhCl]} {/tex} ) are used for alkene hydrogenation.
text_html:
Catalysts in Industry

  • Coordination compounds like Wilkinson catalyst ({tex} ext{[Ph}_3 ext{P}_3 ext{RhCl]} {/tex}) are used for alkene hydrogenation.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Electroplating with Complexes Silver and gold electroplated more smoothly using complex solutions, e.g., $$ ext{[Ag(CN)}_2{]}^{-} $$.
text_html:
Electroplating with Complexes
  • Silver and gold electroplated more smoothly using complex solutions, e.g., $$ ext{[Ag(CN)}_2{]}^{-} $$.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Photography Process Black and white films fixed using hypo solution to form $$ ext{[Ag(S}_2 ext{O}_3 ext{)}_2{]}^{3-} $$.
text_html:
Photography Process
  • Black and white films fixed using hypo solution to form $$ ext{[Ag(S}_2 ext{O}_3 ext{)}_2{]}^{3-} $$.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Medicinal Applications Chelate therapy for removing toxic metals using ligands like D-penicillamine. EDTA is used for treating lead poisoning. Cis-platin used in cancer treatment.
text_html:
Medicinal Applications
  • Chelate therapy for removing toxic metals using ligands like D-penicillamine.
  • EDTA is used for treating lead poisoning.
  • Cis-platin used in cancer treatment.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Importance and Applications of Coordination Compounds
text: Theoretical Insights A. Werner's theory explains coordination compound bonding. Valence Bond Theory discusses formation and shapes. Crystal Field Theory explains electronic arrangements and properties.
text_html:
Theoretical Insights
  • A. Werner's theory explains coordination compound bonding.
  • Valence Bond Theory discusses formation and shapes.
  • Crystal Field Theory explains electronic arrangements and properties.
